scores.note: It finds the load-bearing change—the heap representation rewrite and its Less/Swap hot-path payoff—instead of touring both files evenly.
It walks both a weird-input case (bad indices after guard removal) and an at-scale failure mode (queue/keyIndex desync under churn), and says how each would fail.
It ties ordering, delete/update behavior, and churn claims to named tests and explicitly separates what the tests pin from what remains caller-contract reasoning.
It uses real quantities—O(log n), 100/1000/5000-item benchmarks, 24-46% speedups, and roughly halved allocations—to justify the review's ruling.
It does name X over Y and cost the bookkeeping burden, but the rejected alternative is mostly the status quo rather than a richer competing design.
The close actually rules, names stale queue/keyIndex synchronization as the biggest residual risk, and says what future reviewers should watch.

summary: Pass. This draft interrogates the PR instead of touring it: it isolates the heap rewrite, reasons through how it can fail, and ties approval to named tests plus measured benchmark gains. The thinnest dimension is the trade-off scene, which leans on 'keep the old shape' as the rejected alternative, but the film still clears the bar because the verdict adjudicates a concrete residual risk and watch item.
